coredata.dc:title: The effects of the composition and structure of quantum dots combined with cobalt phthalocyanine and an aptamer on the electrochemical detection of prostate specific antigen
coredata.dc:creator: Nxele, Siphesihle Robin
Nyokong, Tebello
coredata.dc:description: The ability to functionalize and modify graphene-based quantum dots has also allowed for the tuning of their activity in different applications. A comparative study is carried out in this work where a prostate specific aptamer (PSA) and cobalt phthalocyanine molecule are combined with graphene quantum dots (GQDs), nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) and graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots (gCNQDs) in order to determine the effects of quantum dots type (and therefore composition) on the electrocatalytic ability of the sensor to detect the analyte of interest. For the detection of prostate-specific antigen, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is employed to determine detection limits as well as sensitivity and selectivity of the aptasensors. Results showed a good performance of all sensors fabricated with the NGQDs based aptasensor showing the best performance with a detection limit of 1.54 pM in EIS as well as negligible interference in mixed samples. This electrode was further verified in spiked human serum samples and good recovery and low detection limits were determined, putting this aptasensor in good stead as a reliable, efficient PSA aptasensor.

head.ce:title: The effects of the composition and structure of quantum dots combined with cobalt phthalocyanine and an aptamer on the electrochemical detection of prostate specific antigen

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Nanotechnology Innovation, Chemistry Department, Rhodes University, Grahamstown, 6140, South Africa
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Nanotechnology Innovation, Chemistry Department, Rhodes University, Grahamstown, 6140, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The ability to functionalize and modify graphene-based quantum dots has also allowed for the tuning of their activity in different applications. A comparative study is carried out in this work where a prostate specific aptamer (PSA) and cobalt phthalocyanine molecule are combined with graphene quantum dots (GQDs), nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) and graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots (gCNQDs) in order to determine the effects of quantum dots type (and therefore composition) on the electrocatalytic ability of the sensor to detect the analyte of interest. For the detection of prostate-specific antigen, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is employed to determine detection limits as well as sensitivity and selectivity of the aptasensors. Results showed a good performance of all sensors fabricated with the NGQDs based aptasensor showing the best performance with a detection limit of 1.54 pM in EIS as well as negligible interference in mixed samples. This electrode was further verified in spiked human serum samples and good recovery and low detection limits were determined, putting this aptasensor in good stead as a reliable, efficient PSA aptasensor.

Electrochemical aptasensors are developed for the electrochemical detection of prostate specific antigen (PSA) at electrode surfaces modified by immobilizing an aptamer on different types of quantum dots (GQDs, NGQDs and gCNQDs) π-π conjugated to Co phthalocyanine to determine effects of quantum dots composition and structure on the applicability of the sensors.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Early diagnosis of cancers is an important research topic, as it can result in avoiding costly medical treatment for patients, and prevent premature deaths. The electrochemical detection of prostate specific aptamer (PSA), which is a biomarker directly linked to prostate cancer, is a developing early detection method that has gained much attention. This method has shown improved sensitivity, selectivity, simplicity and it is of low-cost [1,2]. Moreover, the method has been found to be less time-consuming owing to the direct binding of a recognition element and biomarker compared to known labelling processes [3]. In addition, incorporating nanomaterials is expected to enhance the response signal and increase sensor stability of these biosensors [1–3]. The current work aims to achieve these characteristics by developing a novel electrochemical aptasensor using a metallophthalocynine and quantum dots (QDs) as the signal and stability enhancing materials, respectively, and an aptamer as the biorecognition element.

Semiconductor QDs (SCQDs) have been used in catalysis. It is possible to combine SCQDs with other materials to make them suit specific biological applications [4–6]. The use of non-metallic graphene quantum dots (pristine and doped) as multi-modal nanoplatforms for sensing has been reported for the electrocatalytic detection of various analytes [7–11]. Graphene quantum dots are easily modifiable, increase surface area of the sensor as well as improve electron transfer abilities. It has also been reported that doping of pristine graphene quantum dots with heteroatoms such as boron [12,13], nitrogen [14–16] and sulfur [17–19] can greatly enhance their electrocatalytic ability towards the detection of a chosen analyte. Graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots (gCNQDs) differ from graphene quantum dots as they have a completely different structure which is predominantly nitrogen-based [20] whereas graphene quantum dots are mainly dominated by carbon atoms unless the structure is altered by functionalization or doping, Fig. 1. gCNQDs, just like other known QDs, show high surface area [21], low toxicity [22] and enhance performance when conjugated to other materials [23,24]. Both types of quantum dots enhance the activity of other electrocatalysts such as metallophthalocyanines (MPc) [25]. Herein, graphene quantum dots, nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots and graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots are each used for the first time together with a prostate-specific antigen (PSA) binding aptamer and CoPc for the detection of PSA in solution using electrochemical methods. PSA tests are used to determine whether a patient has prostate cancer or not [26] because, as already mentioned, early diagnosis may prevent patients from facing more costly, long-term issues and avoid death. Several immunosensors have been fabricated for the detection of PSA using antibodies as the biorecognition elements [27–30]. However, the immobilization of antibodies decreases their activity thus leading to the increasing demand for the use of aptamers [31,32]. Aptamers have high sensitivity, selectivity and stability [33,34], hence a PSA specific aptamer is employed in this work together with a CoPc derivative for the detection of PSA. The CoPc derivative employed (Scheme 1 ) has substituents (tert-butyl) that act as electron donating groups (push) and the single COOH as an electron withdrawing group (pull). The role of the CoPc in this work is to enhance electron transfer as these molecules are known to have good electronic properties [35]. MPc derivatives have been linked to an aptamer for human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2) detection [36] and for biosensing [37]. An MPc has been employed for the photoelectrochemical detection of PSA in the presence of graphene [38], GQDs have also been linked to an aptamer in the presence of a porphyrin (similar in structure to phthalocyanines) for photothermal and photodynamic therapy [39] and GQDs and gCNQDs have been employed for the detection of PSA [40,41]. However, we present the first example of the use the combination of gCNQDs, CoPc and aptamer for the detection of PSA. Combining the CoPc and the QDs enhances the signal response. The PSA specifically binds to the aptamer that is immobilized at the electrode surface. Therefore, when the PSA binds to the aptamer, the signal response should decrease, indicating PSA detection. The elemental composition of the QDs should also play a role on the signal response. Thus, the effects of the different types of QDs (GQDs, NGQDs and gCNQDs) on the activity of the fabricated aptasensors are thus compared, and most importantly, the detection limits and selectivity are compared to determine which quantum dots structure suits the chosen application best for further studies and use of this aptasensor in the early detection and diagnosis of prostate cancer.

Ethylenediaminotetraacetic acid (EDTA), urea, prostate specific antigen (PSA), and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were obtained from Sigma–Aldrich. The synthesis of the push pull Co(II) phthalocyanine has been reported in literature [25]. The synthesis of GQDs and NGQDs have been reported in literature [42]. The synthesis of gCNQDs has also been reported [43]. The amine-functionalized PSA-specific aptamer was synthesized by (and purchased from) Integrated DNA technologies (IDT) and purified using high-performance liquid chromatography and its sequences is listed as follows: single stranded DNA (ssDNA): 5′-NH2-(CH2)6-TTT TTA ATT AAA GCT CGC CAT CAA ATA GCT TT-3′ (235 nmol, >90% purity). A phosphate buffer solution (PBS) of pH 7.4, 10 mM, was employed in this work. Tris-HCl (10 mM, pH 8) was also employed in this work for the storage of the aptamer in solution. The aptamer solution was prepared in a Tris-EDTA buffer as recommended by supplier as follows: A stock solution of pH 8 was prepared by first dissolving the aptamer (1 µmol) in Tris-HCl (1 mL, 1 M) and EDTA (0.2 mL, 0.5 M) followed by topping up with Millipore water to make 100 mL. The stock solution was adjusted accordingly to make a 10 mM buffer solution which was stabilized by adding 10 mM KCl and 10 mM MgCl2, both had a pH of 8. The solution was stored at 4 °C.

Ultra-pure water was obtained from a Milli-Q Water system from ELGA, Veolia water PURELAB, Flex system (Marlow, UK). All other reagents were of analytical grade and used as received from commercial suppliers.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) were recorded using Auto lab potentiostat PGSTAT 302 (Eco Chemie, Utrecht, The Netherlands) driven by the General Purpose Electrochemical system data processing software (GPES, software version 4.9). DPV measurements were recorded at a scan rate of 10 mV/s with a step potential of 10 mV, modulation amplitude of 50 mV, modulation time of 0.05 s and interval time of 0.5 s.

A Zeiss Libra 120 TEM operating at 80 kV and an INCA PENTA FET coupled to the VAGA TESCAM using 20 kV accelerating voltage were used to record transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs. Surface charges (zeta potentials) and dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were investigated using a Malvern Zetasizer Nanoseries, Nano-ZS90.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) experiments were run using an Autolab potentiostat PGSTAT30 equipped with Nova software version 2.1 and measurements were performed between 1 Hz and 10 KHz using a 5 mV rms sinusoidal modulation. EIS was used to measure the changes in capacitance on the substrate surface due to the binding or lack thereof of the PSA.

Scanning electrochemical microscopy (SECM) experiments were carried out using Uniscan Model 370 equipment and a 15 μm Pt microelectrode (Uniscan) as the tip. SECM approach curves were done using the Pt microelectrode with a Pt counter electrode and Ag|AgCl wire as the pseudo-reference electrode. SECM images were obtained by maintaining the tip at a constant Z position and scanning in the X–Y plane over the desired area (constant-height mode of SECM) and monitoring changes in the steady-state current of K3[Fe(CN)6 ] oxidation at −0.1 V vs. Ag|AgCl (as pseudo reference electrode) as the tip travels. Glassy carbon plates (GCP, Goodfellow, UK) of 1 × 1 cm and 2 mm thick were also used as substrates for SECM.
The non-covalent coordination (π–π stacking) between the quantum dots and the CoPc was achieved as follows:

CoPc derivative (10 mg, 0.07 mmol) was dissolved in 2 mL of DMSO, followed by mixing with 5 mg (each) of GQDs, NGQDs, and gCNQDs, in 2 mL of water. The mixtures were then sonicated at room temperature for 1 h. Following that, the mixtures were left to stir at room temperature for 24 h. A mixture of DMSO, water and ethanol (1:5:3) was used to repeatedly wash the product to try get rid of the excess uncoordinated QDs or CoPc. The conjugates are represented as GQDs-CoPc, NGQDs-CoPc and gCNQDs-CoPc.

A three-electrode system comprising of an Ag|AgCl (3 M KCl) as the reference electrode, a platinum (Pt) wire counter electrode and a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) as the working electrode was employed. The method used to modify the electrodes was that of drop-drying. Prior to modification, the electrode surface was cleaned by means of polishing on a SiC-emery paper in slurry made from alumina nano powder (Sigma-Aldrich) and rinsed with Millipore water several times. QDs-CoPc were placed first, on the electrode (using the drop and dry method) followed by aptamer, and the electrodes are represented as GCE-GQDs-CoPc-Aptamer, GCE-NGQDs-CoPc-Aptamer and GCE-gCNQDs-CoPc-Aptamer. The aptamer placed at the surface to be available for PSA binding.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the conjugates of CoPc with QDs
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 shows the conjugation procedure of the QDs with the CoPc and the representation of the QDs employed in the work is shown in Fig. 1. The represented figures are for illustration purposes and not the exact structures of the compounds used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum dots size determination by TEM and DLS
ce:section.ce:para: The sizes of the quantum dots were determined using DLS and TEM. DLS analyses were carried out in solution and TEM analyses were carried out by dissolving the samples then allowing them to dry on a copper grid. Illustrated in Fig. 2 are the results obtained from these two analytical techniques. In Fig. 2 (a), the size of the GQDs was determined as 4.9 nm in TEM and 5 nm in DLS (the latter are listed in Table S1, Supporting Information). TEM shows well dispersed particles which may be a result of repulsion between deprotonated COOH groups possibly present at the surface of these nanostructures. Upon conjugation with CoPc, we see from the DLS plot that the hydrodynamic size increases to 18.2 nm, Table S1. This may be a result of aggregation of the conjugates. In Fig. 2 (b), the size of the NGQDs was determined as 5.9 nm in TEM and 7.5 nm in DLS, Table S1. An increase in hydrodynamic size is also observed upon conjugation of the NGQDs with CoPc, with a size value of 15.7 nm. For gCNQDs, the size in TEM was found to be 7.4 nm and 10.1 nm in DLS. Upon conjugation of gCNQDs with CoPc, there is a size increase to 18.2 nm according to DLS results. Slight discrepancies in NGQDs and gCNQDs particle sizes determined by TEM relative to DLS could be attributed to interference of the dispersant into the hydrodynamic diameter, resulting in larger values for DLS [44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis spectra of Pc, QDs and conjugates
ce:section.ce:para: UV/vis spectroscopy was used in this work to determine the interaction between the CoPc and QDs of interest, Fig. S1 (using gCNQDs and conjugate as examples). QDs alone were dissolved in water (5 mg/mL), the Pc in DMSO (5 mg/mL) and the conjugates (2 mg/mL) in DMSO and water solvent mixture (3:1 v/v). The quantum dots synthesized in this work showed absorption peaks at 348 nm (GQDs), 344 nm (NGQDs) and 331 nm (gCNQDs), Table S1. Upon conjugation with the phthalocyanine, the Q band of the CoPc in gCNQDs-CoPc conjugate is broader compared to CoPc alone, Fig. S1. The broadness is due to aggregation which results in broad or split Q bands, with the high energy band being due to the aggregate and the low energy band due to the monomer. NGQDs-CoPc and GQDs-CoPc showed extensive aggregation with only the peak due to the aggregate being observed. For gCNQDs-CoPc, both the monomer and aggregate peaks are observed and the no change in peak position of the monomer following conjugation, Table S1. Aggregation was also observed above with TEM and DLS.

The loading of the CoPc onto the QDs was investigated following previous studies using absorption instead of fluorescence [45]. This involves comparing the Q band absorbance intensities of the conjugate (QDs-CoPc) with that of the CoPc before the conjugation. Equal masses (mg) for CoPc and QDs-CoPc conjugates were weighed and separately dissolved in the same volume of the solvent. The loading values were determined to be 0.08, 0.39 and 0.21 mg/mg for the GQDs-CoPc, NGQDs-CoPc and gCNQDs-CoPc, respectively, Table S1. The low loading of the Pc on the GQDs could be due to repulsion in solution of deprotonated carboxylic acid groups on the Pc and on the GQDs. The NGQDs and gCNQDs have NH and NH2 functions respectively, which, upon deprotonation become positively charged. Should this occur in solution, there is a possible attraction between the deprotonated COOH groups of the Pc and the deprotonated NH and NH2 groups of the NGQDs and gCNQDs, hence increasing the loading.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of modified electrodes in the absence of PSA

ce:section.ce:para: Cyclic voltammetry was used in order to characterize the modified electrode surfaces at each modification step. The electrodes were first tested in a ferricyanide solution in order to determine the effects the surface composition has on the charge transfer between analytical solution and sensor. The results are summarized in Fig. 3 . The electrodes were also tested in a buffer solution which would later contain the analyte of interest to ensure the absence of interferences during the electrocatalytic detection of the analyte of interest, Fig. 4 . The peaks labelled (I) are due to [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− redox couple in Fig. 3. Peaks at 0.70, labelled (II), could be due to ring-based process in electrodes containing CoPc, clearer in Fig. 3(c). However, the oxidation of amino acids such as guanine which constitute the aptamer also occur in this region also [46].

From the cyclic voltammetry results, the anodic/cathodic peak separations (ΔEp) were determined. The lower ΔEp is related to better charge transfer process. Of the QDs alone, the largest ΔEp is observed for GQDs, showing poor charge transfer, Table 1 . The CoPc alone has a ΔEp of 158 mV, which improves in the presence of NGQDs and gCNGDs, Table 1, showing improved charge transfer. The higher ΔEp for GQDs-CoPc compared to NGQDs-CoPc and gCNQDs-CoPc is a result of the large ΔEp of GQDs alone. Aptamer alone has a large ΔEp of 178 mV. Of the aptamer containing electrodes (QDs-CoPc-Aptamer), the largest ΔEp is observed for surface containing GQDs (361.5 mV) followed by the surface containing gCNQDs (202.6 mV), with the NGQDs containing surface having the lowest ΔEp observed (139.1 mV). The large value for GQDs containing electrodes could be due to the high value of ΔEp of GQDs alone. Thus, in all cases electrodes containing GQDs show the lowest charge transfer compared to those containing NGQDs or gCNQDs.

To compare the surfaces according to the improvement in charge transfer (lower ΔEp) the order (for aptamer containing electrodes) from best to worst is as follows: GCE-NGQDs-CoPc-Aptamer > GCE-gCNQDs-CoPc-Aptamer > GCE-GQDs-CoPc-Aptamer. As discussed in the introduction, the NGQDs are expected to perform better than the GQDs due to the addition of heteroatoms (nitrogen atoms) to the pristine graphitic structure which is known to enhance electrocatalytic activity, therefore higher signal amplification is expected from the NGQDs-based electrode. At first glance, better performance would be expected from the gCNQDs due to their predominantly nitrogen rich structure, however, these results show that it is more likely that the NGQDs have more active sites compared to the gCNQDs which contain cavities between their interlinked triazine groups. With this information, it can be concluded that the type of quantum dots used in combination with the CoPc and aptamer plays a vital role in the functionality of the aptasensor. The high values are likely due to the aptamer blocking the active sites of the quantum dots and the phthalocyanine.

The electrodes were also tested in the buffer solution that will be used as the medium which will contain the analyte of interest. The results are summarized in Fig. 4 (a)–(c). There are no peaks for the bare electrode as expected. The CoIIIPc−2/CoIIPc−2 processes are indicated by the low-intensity peaks near 0 V (II), shifted compared to literature [47]. The peaks labelled (I) which are observed around −0.5 V could be due to CoIIPc−2/CoIPc−2 in CoPc containing electrodes [48], GQDs redox process in GQDs containing electrodes [49], and the reduction of amino acids contained in the Aptamer [50].

From Fig. 3, the Randles-Sevcik Eqn. (1) was applied on [Fe(CN) 6]3-/4 redox couple to determine the effective electrode area, A.

where Ip, A, C, D, n and v are the peak current, the effective surface area, concentration of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4, diffusion coefficient of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4, the number of electrons involved, and scan rate, respectively. The literature value for D = 7.6 × 10−6 cm2s−1 [51] for [Fe(CN)6]3-/4 was used.

By using the effective area of the modified electrodes determined using Eqn. (1) and the total charge determined by integrating the anodic peak (II) areas in Fig. 4 and then by employing Eqn. (2) [52] surface coverages were calculated.
(2)

Q
n F A
where n is the number of electrons transferred (~1), F the Faraday constant (96,485 C mol−1), A is the effective surface area obtained from Eqn. (1) and Г is the surface coverage. The Г values are listed in Table 1. The higher surface coverages imply the increase in the electrode surface area which offers more electrocatalytic surface. By comparing the different electrodes, the highest surface coverage is observed for the electrode contained the gCNQDs, followed by the NGQDs with the lowest being the GQDs-containing electrode surface. The low value for the latter corresponds to the consistently high ΔEp value for GQDs electrodes discussed above.

SECM is a surface analytical technique which was employed to further analyze the conducting behaviour of the modified surfaces studied in this work. A ferricyanide solution was used to carry out the analyses. Fig. 5 shows the approach curves which provide information on the changes in tip current as the ultra-micro electrode (UME) tip approaches the substrate (GCP) in a feedback mode experiment in the presence of Fe2+/Fe3+ redox mediator. The current generated at the tip of the electrode is determined by the substrate, the distance between the tip of the electrode and the substrate as well as the composition of the redox mediator. When the tip is closer to a conductive substrate surface, an increase in the current is observed while an insulating substrate is accompanied by a decline in the current [53,54].

The approach curves obtained for the different surfaces show that the bare GCP as well as the surface modified with the QDs-CoPc conjugate are conducting, with increasing normalized current as the UME approaches the GCP surface. Once the aptamer is incorporated in all cases, we notice a drop in current. The aptamer evidently has a passivating effect on the electrode surface. The area scans are also shown in Fig. S2 for the bare GCP, GCP-QDs-CoPc and the GCP-QDs-CoPc -Aptamer. For GQDs-CoPc, there is a slight reduction in current of the modified region compared to the bare GCP, which may indicate passivation of the GCP surface upon modification, confirming ΔEp values. When the aptamer is incorporated in GQDs-CoPc-Aptamer surfaces, the surface conductivity is further passivated which correlates with the results obtained for the approach curves.

For NGQDs-CoPc, there is an enhancement in surface conductivity compared to bare GCP, contradicting ΔEp values. The current decreased however, upon immobilization of the aptamer which was expected as the same was observed on the approach curves. For gCNQDs-CoPc and gCNQDs-CoPc-Aptamer, there is an enhancement in current compared to bare contradicting ΔEp values These contradiction may arise due to the nature of electrodes being used as well as the area in which the analysis is taking place, where greater aggregation may have occurred on the GCE surface compared to the analyzed area on the GC plate. In SECM, a GC plate is modified and a UME is used to analyze the conductivity of a specific area on the substrate in solution. With CV, the electrode is modified and used as the working electrode to determine extent of electron transfer between substrate and solution. These differences may be the reason contradictions are observed.

The electron transfer kinetics in presence of PSA was determined using EIS. Only the Aptamer containing electrodes were employed. The Nyquist plots Fig. 6 (a) were used to determine Rct values by fitting the Randles equivalent circuit (Fig. 6(c)). Low Rct values indicate improved charge transfer. The Rct values are summarized in Table 2 . The highest Rct values were observed for the NGQDs-based electrode followed by the GQDs-based electrode, with the gCNQDs-based electrode having the lowest values. A higher Rct suggests more PSA binding hence better detection activity. Thus, the electrode containing NGQDs show better PSA detection. This electrode also had a low ΔE in the 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution before incubation with PSA.

The apparent charge transfer rate constant (kapp) was calculated using Eqn. (3) [55] and summarized in Table 2.

where C is the concentration of the PSA, A = real surface area in cm2 and other symbols retain their usual scientific meaning. The real surface area was obtained using Eqn. (1). kapp values were high where RCT were low as expected. The gCNQDs-CoPc-Aptamer combination had the highest kapp value (1.78 × 10−1 cm/s) which implies a less passivated electrode surface compared to the other two electrodes hence minimal binding of PSA to the aptamer or more exposure of the conductive regions of the electrode surface compared to the GQDs and NGQDs-based surfaces. This means the gCNQDs-CoPc-Aptamer is less effective at detecting PSA, even though it has a larger surface coverage, Table 1. This could mean that the greater the surface coverage, the less efficient the binding of the aptamer at the surface of the electrode. Increasing the surface coverage may compromise the possibility of the PSA binding to the aptamer.

A plot of phase-shift against log frequency Fig. 6(b) provides further characteristic information on the frequency which cannot be obtained from the Nyquist plot. All electrodes showed the phase angle less than the ideal 90° indicative of a true capacitor (Table 2) [56]. Shift of phase angles towards lower frequencies in the Bode plots are an indication that the electrocatalyst gives more efficient catalytic activity [57]. The Bode plots show complicated behaviours with broad peaks suggesting complicated relaxation behaviour. This has been observed before and related many factors including film thickness [58], which could result in different diffusion processes within the film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Concentration studies using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS)
ce:section.ce:para: For the determination of limits of detection (LoD), the aptamer-containing electrodes were tested in a 30 mM phosphate buffer solution of pH 7.4 with PSA concentrations ranging from 1.2 to 2.0 pM. The analytical techniques used were DPV and EIS. However, the DPV peaks were too broad (Fig. S3 (Supporting information)) for accurate determination of LoD values.

Differential pulse voltammograms shown in Fig. S3 show more than one prominent peak in a potential window between −0.7 V and 0 V. Peak I was observed at −0.6 V for GQDs-based surface and −0.7 V (I) for the NGQDs and gCNQDs based surfaces [49]. The DPV plots of the electrode containing aptamer alone (GCE-Aptamer, Fig. S3(d)) shows two peaks between 0 V and −0.25, which are known for DNA [50]. These peaks are likely to overlap with the CoIIIPc−2/CoIIPc−2 processes for electrodes containing CoPc. Peaks II and III were observed near −0.25 V and 0 V, respectively for all surfaces.

Fig. 7 shows the Nyquist plots of the electrodes in PSA solutions of different concentrations. The detection limits determined in EIS are also summarized in Table 2. The GQDs, NGQDs and gCNQDs-based electrodes showed detection limits of 3.48, 1.54 and 11.59 pM. The gCNQDs shows the highest LoD in EIS with the NGQDs performing the best.

The selectivity, stability and reproducibility of the fabricated electrochemical aptasensors were investigated using DPV. The obtained voltammograms for stability and reproducibility studies are summarized as supporting information, captioned under Fig. S4 and Fig. S5 respectively. The calculated results are summarized in Table 3 . For reproducibility studies, the same electrode was fabricated on three different occasions (labelled as 1,2,3 on the figures) and tested in the PSA solution. The relative standard deviation (RSD (%)) was determined from peaks at 0 V for GQDs, and was found to be 3.8%. For the NGQDs based electrode, the RSD (%) was determined from peaks at −0.3 V and was found to be 6.2%. For the gCNQDs based electrode, the RSD (%) was determined from peaks at 0 V, and was found to be 6.7%. When the electrode was not in use, it was stored at 4 οC. After a week of storage, the aptasensors were used to detect PSA in PBS solution which allowed for the determination of the stability of the aptasensors. By looking at the difference between peak currents, the GQDs, NGQDs and gCNQDs based electrodes maintained approximately 89%, 95.3% and 92.2% of their performance ability respectively, proving to be quite stable in that time frame. Table 3 summarizes the LoD, repeatability and reproducibility results obtained in this work, which are further compared to other literature reports related to the detection of PSA [59–64]. The aptasensor reported in this work showed the lowest detection limit and one of the lowest reproducibility and repeatability RSD%. From this it can be concluded that these aptasensors have good potential to be used in clinical studies.

The mixed solution method [65] and DPV (Fig. S6) was used to test the selectivity of the developed aptasensors in complex mixtures, to prove their selectivity towards PSA. The chosen test analytes were BSA, l-cysteine and glucose which were mixed, in excess, in the buffer solution with PSA. Either than being easily accessible in the laboratory, BSA was chosen so as to determine how other proteins expressed in biological samples could affect the aptasensor functionality. Glucose was chosen as it is possible to detect it in seminal samples and level may vary depending on the health of the patient. l-cysteine was chosen as a test analyte as it is an amino acid which can be found in the human body or a variant thereof may be ingested as a supplement to enhance sperm quality [66]. The concentration of PSA used was 2.0 pM and 2.0 nM of interferant analyte was added for non-specific binding determination. The interferents were added in access (×10−3 higher than analyte) to ensure a high enough concentration for the sensor to pick up should it be sensitive to the interferences. The fabricated electrodes were incubated in the solutions as was done for PSA studies in other sections of this work. Eqn. (4) was used to determine values of the selectivity coefficients (Kamp) which will give a measure of the degree of interference.
(4)
K AMP
=
(
I MIXTURE
I PSA
− 1
)
[ PSA ]
[ INTERFERANT ]
where Imixture and IPSA are background corrected current responses of PSA in the presence and absence of the interferent, respectively. A Kamp value in the order of magnitude higher than 10−3 indicates strong interference and a value close to 10−3 indicates weak interference [65]. The peak potentials employed to calculate the Kamp values are −0.25 V (II) (for NGQDs and gCNQDs) and 0 V (III) (for GQDs and gCNQDs) since they were more resolved. The calculated values were summarized in Table 4 . A Kamp value in the order of magnitude higher than 10−3 indicates strong interference and a value close to 10−3 indicates weak interference [65]. Table 4 shows values close or lower than a magnitude of 10−3 which indicates low interference and high selectivity of the fabricated sensors towards PSA detection. The gCNQDs-based electrodes also seem to show no interference at the most negative potential which indicates excellent potential as early detection devices towards PSA and thus reliable early diagnosis of prostate cancer.

The detection of PSA in PBS solutions showed that the NGQDs-based aptasensor performed the best compared to the GQDs and gCNQDs-based aptasensors in terms of LOD. There could be a number of reasons for this behaviour. As mentioned in the introduction, introducing heteroatoms to the pristine GQDs structure is known to improve the electrocatalytic ability of the nanostructures which means the signal response of the sensor is improved, therefore the NGQDs are expected to perform better than the GQDs. The gCNQDs on the other hand do contain nitrogen atoms and have also been reported to have a large surface area, however, the cavities present within the graphitic structure may be the cause of the shortfall of the activity of these nanostructures compared to the NGQDs which are still as robust as the GQDs but contain nitrogen atoms which cause surface defects that lead to enhanced signal output. Moreover, reports have shown that gCNQDs perform better as hybrids, combined with other semiconducting nanostructures [21,22,24].

The reliability and feasibility of the fabricated GCE-NGQDs-CoPc-Aptamer aptasensor was therefore tested in spiked human serum samples using EIS. The serum samples were diluted 100-fold with PBS (pH 7.4) in order to reduce the matrix effect of the serum. Concentrations of PSA in the range of 1.2–2.0 pM were employed. EIS was used as the analytical tool to detect PSA in the spiked human serum samples for the determination of the LoD. The obtained responses in EIS are shown in Fig. 8 . The LoD was determined as 0.043 ng/mL which is similar to that obtained for PSA in buffer (0.044 ng/mL) and is still below the cut-off value of 4 ng/mL [67] of patients suspected to have prostate cancer.

The verification of the reliability and feasibility of this aptasensor was also done by calculating the % recovery in the concentration range of 1.2–2 pM, using EIS. The recovery was calculated as the ratio of the sensor performance in spiked human serum samples to that obtained in buffer for the same concentrations of PSA as reported in literature [68]. The results obtained are shown in Table 5 . The amount of PSA found in spiked serum was determined by calculating the percentage signal based on PSA detection in the chosen buffer. All experiments were run in triplicate and the recorded values are an average of the values recorded for each run. The percentage recovery calculated was 85.9% at the lowest concentration level of 1.2 pM and 93.7% at the highest concentration level of 2 pM. This shows that the results obtained from detecting PSA in buffer are feasible and reliable as they are in accordance with analyses done in PBS. Although the results obtained from EIS indicate some interference due to the complexity of the serum which resulted in a decrease in sensitivity (i.e.reduced Rct values obtained in serum analyses compared to those done in the buffer solution), the change is not drastic.

After investigating how the composition and the structure of the reported quantum dots, in combination with CoPc and an aptamer could affect the functionality of the developed aptasensor, the NGQDs based electrode proved to have the lowest detection limits compared to the other aptasensors. The NGQDs are known to perform better in electrocatalysis compared to the GQDs due to the introduction of nitrogen atoms into the graphitic structure so their better behavior compared to the GQDs was expected. The detection limits determined from impedance studies were in agreement with the RCT values determined regarding the order of best performance of the aptasensors. The assumption was that the higher the RCT value, the better the PSA binding as the binding of PSA results in passivation of electron transfer at the electrode surface. The fabricated aptasensors also showed good stability, selectivity and were reproducible. The feasibility and reliability of the results obtained in this work were further verified by detecting PSA in real human serum samples using the NGQDs-based aptasensor. The % recoveries obtained using EIS were in accordance with the data obtained for analysis of PSA in PBS solution which is a good indicator that the aptasensor fabricated in this work can be used in clinical studies. Moreover, the detection limit determined using EIS was comparable to the LoD obtained for the best performing aptasensor in PBS. With the combination of these QDs with CoPc and an aptamer for the detection of PSA being a novel study, it was important to determine how changing the nature of the quantum dots would affect the entire system and this study has proven that the composition and structure has noticeable effects and therefore makes this work important as a preliminary study to the further development of these aptasensors and their application in clinical analyses.
Siphesihle Robin Nxele: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Tebello Nyokong: Supervision, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.
